title: PRD: Phase 3 Git Status Sidebar
description:
## Problem

Argus users working in project workspaces need a fast, always-visible view of repository state without leaving the terminal. Today the right sidebar is only a placeholder, so users must run git commands manually to understand changed files, staging state, branch tracking, and diffs. This breaks flow, makes multi-worktree development harder to scan, and hides important context from the titlebar.

## Solution

Add the Phase 3 git status sidebar: a toggleable right-side panel that follows the active workspace's git root, refreshes automatically as files change, summarizes branch/upstream state, groups changed files by staging state, and exposes common file operations with safe confirmations. The sidebar should make routine git review and staging work possible from Argus while keeping the terminal as the primary workspace.

## User Stories

1. As an Argus user, I want the right sidebar to show the git status of my active workspace, so that I can see repository changes without running git commands.
2. As an Argus user, I want the sidebar to track the workspace's worktree root rather than the terminal's live directory, so that git status is stable and predictable before shell integration exists.
3. As an Argus user, I want main-checkout workspaces to use the project repository root, so that project root status is shown for the primary checkout.
4. As an Argus user, I want standalone or non-repository workspaces to show a clear not-repository state, so that I understand why no git status is available.
5. As an Argus user, I want a way to initialize a repository from the not-repository state, so that I can start tracking a workspace when appropriate.
6. As an Argus user, I want the sidebar to show my current branch, so that I know which branch I am modifying.
7. As an Argus user, I want the sidebar to show upstream tracking information, so that I know whether I am ahead or behind the remote.
8. As an Argus user, I want changed files grouped into staged, unstaged, and untracked sections, so that I can understand what will be committed.
9. As an Argus user, I want each section to show a file count, so that I can assess change volume at a glance.
10. As an Argus user, I want sections to be collapsible, so that I can focus on the part of the working tree I am editing.
11. As an Argus user, I want each file row to show an appropriate status icon and color, so that I can distinguish added, modified, deleted, renamed, copied, type-changed, and untracked files quickly.
12. As an Argus user, I want each changed file to show added and removed line counts, so that I can estimate review size.
13. As an Argus user, I want untracked directories expanded into child files, so that I can stage or delete individual new files.
14. As an Argus user, I want very large repositories to cap the displayed file list, so that the app remains responsive.
15. As an Argus user, I want a loading indicator during refresh, so that I know the sidebar is updating rather than stale.
16. As an Argus user, I want a manual refresh button, so that I can force an update when needed.
17. As an Argus user, I want status to refresh automatically after filesystem changes, so that the sidebar stays current during normal editing.
18. As an Argus user, I want automatic refresh to ignore git-internal file churn and avoid rapid loops, so that the UI does not flicker or waste CPU.
19. As an Argus user, I want status to refresh immediately after any file operation, so that the sidebar reflects the result of my action.
20. As an Argus user, I want staged files to offer unstage, diff, blame, and copy-path actions, so that I can manage committed content efficiently.
21. As an Argus user, I want unstaged files to offer stage, discard, diff, blame, and copy-path actions, so that I can prepare or revert local edits.
22. As an Argus user, I want untracked files to offer stage, delete, diff, and copy-path actions, so that I can either keep or remove new files.
23. As an Argus user, I want destructive actions to require confirmation, so that I do not accidentally lose work.
24. As an Argus user, I want section-level bulk actions, so that I can stage, unstage, discard, or delete many files without repetitive clicks.
25. As an Argus user, I want bulk destructive actions to require confirmation, so that large irreversible changes are intentional.
26. As an Argus user, I want a diff preview, so that I can inspect changes before staging, discarding, or committing.
27. As an Argus user, I want a blame preview, so that I can see line ownership/history for a file without leaving Argus.
28. As an Argus user, I want preview panels to be dismissible with Escape or a close button, so that they do not interrupt flow.
29. As an Argus user, I want preview panels to appear near the main window and stay on screen, so that they feel connected to the file I opened.
30. As an Argus user, I want a clean working tree state, so that I know when no changes require attention.
31. As an Argus user, I want the titlebar to show branch, dirty state, and ahead/behind counts, so that workspace git state is visible even when the sidebar is closed.
32. As an Argus user, I want git operations to run off the main thread, so that terminals and UI remain responsive.
33. As an Argus user, I want errors from git commands to be presented clearly and non-destructively, so that I can recover without losing context.

## Implementation Decisions

- Build a git status domain model that represents repository availability, branch name, upstream relationship, ahead/behind counts, clean state, capped file groups, per-file status, paths, and diff statistics.
- Build a deep, isolated porcelain parser for `git status --porcelain=v2 --branch`. It should expose a small parsing interface and hide git output quirks from UI code.
- Build a deep, isolated diff-stat parser for numeric diff output. It should handle binary files and missing stats without leaking parsing details to the sidebar.
- Build a git status service that shells out to the git CLI for all reads and writes. It should be non-main-actor and safe to call asynchronously.
- Use the workspace's worktree root as the status root. For main-checkout project workspaces, use the project repository root. Do not follow terminal live cwd.
- Keep git status behavior independent of Ghostty terminal state; terminal focus changes should only affect which workspace is active.
- Add a filesystem watcher abstraction backed by recursive macOS FSEvents, not single-file descriptor watching.
- Configure filesystem refresh behavior with at least 300 ms debounce, `.git` event filtering, and at least a 1 s cooldown after refresh completion.
- Ensure automatic refresh cancellation or root switching is correct when the active workspace changes.
- Add a view model/controller layer between workspace selection, status service, watcher, and sidebar UI so SwiftUI views remain declarative.
- Replace the placeholder right sidebar with a full git sidebar that preserves existing toggle and resize behavior.
- Represent staged, unstaged, and untracked as separate collapsible sections with stable section state.
- Enforce a display cap of 500 files across sidebar file rows to prevent slow rendering.
- Expand untracked directories into child file entries for display and operations.
- Implement file operations through git and filesystem commands with explicit action types: stage, unstage, discard, delete, copy path, diff, and blame.
- Require confirmation before discard, delete, discard-all, and delete-all operations.
- Refresh status immediately after every completed file operation, regardless of filesystem watcher timing.
- Implement section-level bulk operations for stage all, unstage all, discard all, and delete all.
- Implement diff preview using colorized git diff output and blame preview using colorized git blame output; use difftastic for diff only if available.
- Display previews in floating utility panels that can close via Escape or a close button and are clamped to visible screen bounds.
- Update titlebar state from the same git status source used by the sidebar, so sidebar and titlebar do not disagree.
- Surface clean and not-repository states as first-class UI states rather than errors.
- Provide an initialize-repository action for not-repository roots.
- Treat git command failures as recoverable UI state and avoid app crashes.

## Testing Decisions

- Good tests should verify external behavior and user-visible outcomes, not private implementation details. Parser tests should assert parsed domain objects for representative command outputs. Service tests should assert repository state after real git operations in temporary repositories. UI-oriented tests should assert visible behavior and action availability.
- Test the porcelain parser with clean repositories, branch headers, upstream ahead/behind data, staged changes, unstaged changes, untracked files, renamed files, copied files, deleted files, type changes, and paths with spaces.
- Test diff-stat parsing for additions, deletions, binary files, missing paths, renamed paths, and files that appear in multiple sections.
- Test root resolution for worktree workspaces, main-checkout project workspaces, standalone git directories, and non-repository directories.
- Test git status service behavior in temporary repositories using the git CLI, including clean state, staged/unstaged/untracked grouping, branch metadata, and capped results.
- Test file operations with temporary repositories: stage, unstage, discard, delete untracked, bulk stage, bulk unstage, bulk discard, and bulk delete.
- Test that destructive operations are not executed until confirmation is accepted.
- Test that automatic refresh coalesces rapid filesystem events, filters `.git` changes, respects cooldown, and switches watched roots when the active workspace changes.
- Test that manual refresh and post-operation refresh update the exposed status state.
- Test sidebar UI states for loading, clean, not-repository, error, capped-file warning, collapsed sections, and available row actions.
- Test titlebar git metadata for branch display, dirty indicator, and ahead/behind display.
- Reuse the existing pattern of focused Swift unit tests for domain logic and services, plus the existing Python UI smoke-test style for high-level app behavior.

## Out of Scope

- Shell integration for tracking the terminal's live current directory.
- Commit creation, commit message editing, push, pull, fetch, merge, rebase, and conflict resolution workflows.
- A full in-app diff editor or staging individual hunks.
- Multi-window behavior beyond the existing single-window v1 constraints.
- Browser-panel git behavior beyond using the active workspace's git root.
- Persistent session restoration of transient git status or watcher state.
- Localization, analytics, crash reporting, or distributed-app hardening.

## Further Notes

Phase 3 should prioritize responsiveness and correctness over broad git feature coverage. The status parser, watcher, and operations service are the key deep modules: each should hide messy platform or git details behind small interfaces that are easy to test without involving SwiftUI. The authoritative behavior comes from the Argus specification, with the implementation plan providing sequencing and rationale.

title: Phase 3: Active workspace git summary sidebar
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-9hp`.
Reference user stories: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 15, 16, 30, 32, 33.

## What to build

Create the first demoable git sidebar path for the active workspace: resolve the correct git root, run git status off the main thread, parse branch/upstream/clean/not-repository/error state, and replace the placeholder sidebar with a real loading/summary/empty-section UI plus manual refresh. This slice should not implement per-file rows or file operations yet, but it must establish the shared status state future slices will extend.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] The right sidebar in `MainWindowView` uses a real git status view instead of `GitSidebarPlaceholder`, while preserving `GitSidebarState` visibility and width behavior.
- [ ] The status root is the workspace `worktreePath` for worktree workspaces, the owning project `repositoryPath` for main-checkout workspaces, and the workspace `currentDirectory` for standalone workspaces; it never follows terminal live cwd.
- [ ] A non-main-actor git status service shells out to `/usr/bin/git` using the existing `WorktreeService` process-spawning style and never blocks SwiftUI rendering.
- [ ] The service parses `git status --porcelain=v2 --branch` enough to expose branch name, upstream name, ahead/behind counts, clean/not-repository/error state, and empty staged/unstaged/untracked section counts.
- [ ] The sidebar displays loading, clean working tree, not-repository, recoverable error, and branch/upstream summary states.
- [ ] The manual refresh button updates the displayed state for the active workspace.
- [ ] Tests cover root resolution, branch/upstream parsing, clean state, not-repository state, and manual refresh behavior with temporary repositories where practical.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately.

## Notes for future agents

Relevant integration points: `.specs/argus-application.md` Git Status Sidebar and Git File Operations sections; `docs/argus-implementation-plan.md` Phase 3; `Argus/Views/MainWindowView.swift`; `Argus/Views/Content/GitSidebarPlaceholder.swift`; `Argus/Views/Sidebar/SidebarState.swift`; `Argus/Services/WorkspaceManager.swift`; `Argus/Services/WorktreeService.swift`; `Argus/Views/Titlebar/TitlebarView.swift`. Prefer deep modules for status parsing/root resolution with focused tests under a Phase 3 test target/directory. Keep transient git status out of session persistence.

title: Phase 3: Changed file rows with diff stats and caps
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-9hp`.
Reference user stories: 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 32, 33.

## What to build

Extend the active workspace sidebar from summary-only to a changed-file view: parse porcelain v2 file records, augment them with diff statistics, group rows into staged/unstaged/untracked sections, expand untracked directories into child files, and cap rendered files at 500.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] The sidebar shows staged, unstaged, and untracked sections with stable collapsible state and accurate visible counts.
- [ ] File rows display path, status icon/color, and added/removed line counts for added, modified, deleted, renamed, copied, type-changed, and untracked files.
- [ ] `git diff --numstat` output is parsed and associated with the correct file rows, including binary files and renamed paths.
- [ ] Untracked directories are expanded into individual child file rows for display.
- [ ] The displayed file list is capped at 500 rows and exposes a clear capped-results indication when more files exist.
- [ ] Tests cover porcelain v2 parsing for representative file statuses, diff-stat parsing, untracked directory expansion, and capping behavior.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-9hp.1` — requires the shared active workspace status service/view model and real sidebar shell.

## Notes for future agents

Build on the status domain model created by `argus-9hp.1`. Keep parsing independent from SwiftUI so tests can assert domain objects directly. The authoritative cap is 500 displayed files. Use git CLI commands only; do not introduce libgit2. Preserve the rule that the status root is the workspace/project git root, not terminal cwd.

title: Phase 3: FSEvents auto-refresh for active git root
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-9hp`.
Reference user stories: 15, 17, 18, 32, 33.

## What to build

Add automatic refresh for the active workspace git root using recursive macOS FSEvents. The watcher should follow active workspace changes, coalesce rapid filesystem edits, ignore git-internal churn, and refresh the same sidebar state used by manual refresh.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A filesystem watcher abstraction uses the FSEventStream C API via `FSEventStreamCreate`, not `DispatchSource.makeFileSystemObjectSource`.
- [ ] The watcher recursively monitors the active workspace git root and switches roots when the selected workspace changes.
- [ ] File events trigger a sidebar refresh after at least 300 ms debounce.
- [ ] Events under `.git/` are filtered to prevent feedback loops.
- [ ] After a refresh completes, filesystem-triggered refreshes are suppressed for at least 1 second.
- [ ] Loading state is visible during automatic refreshes and git command failures remain recoverable UI state.
- [ ] Tests or deterministic seams cover root switching, `.git/` filtering, debounce/cooldown behavior, and refresh triggering.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-9hp.1` — requires the shared status refresh path and active workspace status state.

## Notes for future agents

The spec explicitly requires FSEvents with 300 ms debounce and 1 s cooldown. Keep platform callback code isolated behind a small interface so debounce/cooldown logic can be tested without relying on real filesystem timing. Watcher state is transient and must not be persisted.

title: Phase 3: Initialize repository from not-repo state
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-9hp`.
Reference user stories: 4, 5, 30, 33.

## What to build

Make the not-repository sidebar state actionable: when the active workspace root is not a git repository, offer to initialize a repository, run the operation safely, and refresh into a clean repository state.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Non-repository roots show a clear not-repository state instead of a generic error.
- [ ] The not-repository state offers an initialize-repository action.
- [ ] Initializing invokes git through the same async git service/process-spawning approach as other git operations.
- [ ] After successful initialization, the sidebar refreshes and shows a clean repository on the current branch where git provides one.
- [ ] Initialization failures are presented as recoverable UI state and do not crash or block the app.
- [ ] Tests cover not-repository detection, successful initialization in a temporary directory, post-init refresh, and failure handling.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-9hp.1` — requires not-repository detection and refreshable sidebar state.

## Notes for future agents

Keep this narrow: only initialize a repository. Do not add commit, remote, push, pull, or branch-management workflows. Preserve the product language from the spec: not-repository should offer repository initialization rather than displaying an unrecoverable error.

- [x] The not-repository state offers an initialize-repository action.
- [x] Initializing invokes git through the same async git service/process-spawning approach as other git operations.
- [x] After successful initialization, the sidebar refreshes and shows a clean repository on the current branch where git provides one.
- [x] Initialization failures are presented as recoverable UI state and do not crash or block the app.
- [x] Tests cover not-repository detection, successful initialization in a temporary directory, post-init refresh, and failure handling.

title: Phase 3: Stage, unstage, and copy-path file actions
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-9hp`.
Reference user stories: 19, 20, 21, 22, 32, 33.

## What to build

Add the first row-level file operations: stage unstaged/untracked files, unstage staged files, and copy file paths. These actions should be available from the file rows, run asynchronously, and immediately refresh the sidebar when complete.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Staged file rows expose unstage and copy-path actions.
- [ ] Unstaged file rows expose stage and copy-path actions.
- [ ] Untracked file rows expose stage and copy-path actions.
- [ ] Stage and unstage operations invoke git from the status root and update repository state correctly.
- [ ] Copy-path copies the expected path without mutating git state.
- [ ] The sidebar refreshes immediately after stage or unstage completes, independent of filesystem watcher timing.
- [ ] Git operation failures surface as recoverable UI state.
- [ ] Tests cover stage, unstage, copy-path, post-operation refresh, and action availability per section.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-9hp.2` — requires parsed file rows and section-aware sidebar UI.

## Notes for future agents

This slice intentionally excludes destructive actions, bulk actions, diff preview, and blame preview. Use the same root-resolution rule and async git command discipline as earlier Phase 3 work. Avoid shelling through a user shell; invoke git directly via `Process`.

title: PRD: Phase 4 Session Persistence
description:
## Problem

Argus currently restores only enough state to reopen basic workspaces. For daily use, that is not enough: quitting or relaunching the app can lose tab focus, split layout, scrollback context, sidebar state, panel-specific state, and future browser panel state. Users need to trust that their terminal workspace will come back exactly enough to continue work without manual reconstruction, while avoiding unsafe or stale runtime restoration.

## Solution

Argus will save and restore a complete versioned session snapshot for the single main window. The restored session will preserve projects, workspaces, ordered tabs, focused panel, split layouts, terminal working directories, bounded terminal scrollback, browser panel state, and sidebar visibility/width. Saves will happen automatically during typing-quiet periods and synchronously on quit. Restores will happen on launch only when safe and intended; incompatible, empty, oversized, test, argument-based, or explicitly disabled restores will start fresh.

## User Stories

1. As an Argus user, I want my projects and workspaces restored after relaunch, so that I can resume my workspace organization without rebuilding it.
2. As an Argus user, I want each workspace's tab order restored, so that my muscle memory for tabs remains intact.
3. As an Argus user, I want the active workspace and active tab restored, so that relaunch returns me to the work I was last doing.
4. As an Argus user, I want terminal panels to reopen in their last known directories, so that new shell sessions start in the right context.
5. As an Argus user, I want split-pane layouts restored, so that complex terminal arrangements survive restart.
6. As an Argus user, I want terminal scrollback restored up to a large limit, so that recent command output and agent logs remain available after restart.
7. As an Argus user, I want restored scrollback to preserve colors and avoid malformed terminal control bytes, so that restored terminal output is readable and safe.
8. As an Argus user, I want browser panels to remember their URL, zoom, and developer tools state, so that browser workflows can resume when browser panels exist.
9. As an Argus user, I want sidebar visibility and widths restored, so that the window layout matches my preferred setup.
10. As an Argus user, I want Argus to autosave during quiet periods, so that crashes or forced restarts lose little state.
11. As an Argus user, I want Argus to save synchronously when quitting, so that normal quit never races against session persistence.
12. As an Argus user, I want launch arguments to suppress restore, so that opening Argus for a file or URL does not replace that intent with an old session.
13. As an Argus user, I want an environment variable to disable restore, so that scripts and troubleshooting can force a clean session.
14. As a test runner, I want automated tests to skip session restore, so that tests are deterministic and isolated from user state.
15. As an Argus user, I want incompatible session files discarded rather than partially interpreted, so that schema changes do not corrupt the running session.
16. As an Argus user, I want empty or oversized snapshots rejected, so that corrupted data cannot create an unusable app state.
17. As an Argus user, I do not want stale agent statuses, process IDs, or other ephemeral runtime state restored, so that the UI does not show dead processes as active.

## Implementation Decisions

- Introduce a dedicated session persistence boundary that owns encoding, decoding, validation, save policy, restore policy, and file I/O rather than keeping full persistence embedded in workspace orchestration.
- The session snapshot will use one schema version. No schema migration will be implemented; version mismatch discards the snapshot and starts fresh.
- The top-level snapshot will model an array of windows for forward compatibility, while v1 writes and accepts exactly one window.
- The schema will enforce a maximum of 12 windows and 128 workspaces per window; v1 runtime remains single-window.
- Project snapshots will include stable identifiers, names, repository paths, main branches, expand/collapse state, workspace identifiers, optional colors, and catch-all status.
- Workspace snapshots will include project association, worktree path, worktree branch, display title state, selected panel, ordered tabs, and panel layout state.
- Panel snapshots will be typed. Terminal panel snapshots will capture surface identity, working directory, title metadata, and scrollback payload. Browser panel snapshots will capture current URL, zoom level, and developer tools visibility.
- Sidebar state will be part of the application snapshot, including left sidebar visibility/width and right git sidebar visibility/width.
- Terminal scrollback capture will be encapsulated behind a small interface so truncation and ANSI-boundary handling can be tested independently from Ghostty rendering.
- Terminal scrollback is capped at 400,000 characters per terminal panel.
- When truncating scrollback, the system will avoid starting replay inside a partial ANSI escape sequence and will wrap truncated replay text in ANSI reset codes.
- Autosave will run approximately every 8 seconds only after quiet periods, avoiding excessive writes while the user is actively typing or the terminal is producing high-volume output.
- Quit save will be synchronous and must complete before app termination continues.
- Restore will be skipped when explicitly disabled by environment, when launched with file or URL arguments, or when running in automated test environments.
- Ephemeral runtime state such as agent status entries, agent PIDs, notifications, transient loading state, and live process identity will not be restored.

## Testing Decisions

- Tests should verify externally observable behavior: given a running app state, saving and restoring produces the expected user-visible session state; given invalid input, the app starts fresh.
- Pure snapshot tests will cover Codable round trips, schema version rejection, empty snapshot rejection, window/workspace caps, one-window enforcement, and project/workspace reconciliation.
- Scrollback tests will cover the 400,000-character cap, ANSI escape boundary truncation, reset wrapping, empty scrollback, plain text, and mixed control sequences.
- Restore-policy tests will cover environment-disabled restore, automated-test restore suppression, launch-argument restore suppression, incompatible snapshot fallback, and oversized snapshot fallback.
- Autosave tests will use controllable clocks/schedulers or test seams rather than wall-clock sleeps where possible.
- Workspace restoration tests will build on existing Phase 2 session, reconciliation, terminal directory, split shortcut, and tab-order tests.
- Sidebar persistence tests will build on existing sidebar state tests and verify both left and git sidebar state through the session snapshot.
- Git status and agent-related tests should confirm that ephemeral status-like runtime state is absent from restored sessions when those systems exist.

## Out of Scope

- Schema migrations between multiple persistence versions.
- Multi-window runtime support; only the schema is forward-compatible.
- Restoring live shell processes, process IDs, agent state, or command execution state.
- Browser panel UI implementation beyond the persistence contract required for browser panel state.
- Changing git status refresh behavior or worktree management semantics.
- Replacing Ghostty configuration persistence; terminal settings continue to come from Ghostty-compatible config.

## Further Notes

Phase 4 should replace the minimal earlier persistence with a durable full-session model. The design should stay simple for a personal single-machine app, but the persistence boundary should be deep enough to test independently and protect against data loss.

title: Phase 4: Round-trip a one-window session skeleton
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-o2m`.
Reference user stories: 1, 2, 3, 4, 9, 15, 16.

## What to build

Replace the current minimal Phase 2 persistence path with a full-session skeleton that saves and restores one Argus window end to end. The slice should cover the top-level session envelope, one-window schema, project/workspace reconciliation, selected workspace restoration, basic terminal panel snapshots, and sidebar visibility/width in a durable `SessionManager`-style boundary. Existing app behavior should still start with a fresh catch-all workspace when no valid snapshot exists.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Saving a session with projects, catch-all workspace membership, selected workspace, terminal tab count/directories, and left/right sidebar state produces a versioned snapshot with exactly one window.
- [ ] Restoring that snapshot recreates the same user-visible project/workspace/sidebar state and selected workspace.
- [ ] Incompatible schema versions, empty workspace lists, more than one v1 window, more than 12 schema windows, or more than 128 workspaces in the window are rejected by starting fresh.
- [ ] The existing Phase 2 reconciliation behavior for project/workspace cross-references remains covered.
- [ ] Tests cover Codable round trip, invalid snapshot fallback, and integration through `WorkspaceManager` rather than only isolated structs.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately.

## Notes for future agents

Relevant current files include `Argus/Models/SessionSnapshot.swift`, `Argus/Services/WorkspaceManager.swift`, `Argus/Models/Workspace.swift`, `Argus/Models/Project.swift`, and `Argus/Views/Sidebar/SidebarState.swift`. Existing prior art lives in `Tests/Phase2ProjectWorktreeTests/SessionSnapshotTests.swift`, `SessionReconciliationTests.swift`, `TerminalDirectorySnapshotTests.swift`, and `Tests/Phase1FoundationTests/SidebarStatePersistenceTests.swift`. Keep the PRD rule: one schema version, no migrations, v1 runtime single-window, schema cap 12 windows, 128 workspaces per window.

title: Phase 4: Restore panel topology and focus per workspace
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-o2m`.
Reference user stories: 2, 3, 4, 5.

## What to build

Extend the session skeleton so each workspace restores its complete terminal panel topology: ordered top-level tabs, selected/focused panel, active tab, split-pane layout tree, terminal working directories, and durable panel/surface identifiers where safe. The restored workspace should look and behave like the saved workspace before scrollback is considered.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A workspace with multiple terminal tabs restores the same tab order and active tab.
- [ ] A workspace with vertical and horizontal split panes restores the same pane tree and focused pane.
- [ ] Closing/reopening from a snapshot preserves terminal panel working directories for both tabs and split panes.
- [ ] Restored panel IDs and layout references are internally consistent; dangling layout leaves are discarded or cause a fresh safe fallback for that workspace.
- [ ] Tests build on existing terminal tab ordinal and split shortcut tests and assert external workspace behavior, not private storage details.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-o2m.1` — full-session envelope and restore boundary must exist before panel topology can plug into it.

## Notes for future agents

Current topology state is in `Workspace.panelOrder`, `Workspace.panels`, `Workspace.activePanelId`, and `Workspace.tabLayouts`. `PanelLayoutNode` is not currently Codable; add a durable snapshot representation rather than leaking implementation details into the session format if that keeps the model cleaner. Preserve the app's current tab UX: tab labels remain ordinal (`Tab 1`, `Tab 2`, ...), and split panes do not create top-level tabs.

title: Phase 4: Enforce launch restore policy and safe fallback
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-o2m`.
Reference user stories: 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17.

## What to build

Make launch-time restore conditional on the spec's restore policy and verify invalid snapshots fall back to a fresh session without crashing. Restore must be skipped when disabled by environment, when running under automated tests, or when launched with file/URL arguments. Runtime-only state must stay out of snapshots and restored sessions.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `ARGUS_DISABLE_SESSION_RESTORE=1` starts a fresh session even when a valid snapshot exists.
- [ ] Automated test environments start fresh and do not read the user's session file.
- [ ] File or URL launch arguments suppress restore and start from the explicit launch intent instead of the old session.
- [ ] Invalid JSON, incompatible schema, empty snapshots, oversized snapshots, and invalid window counts are handled by starting fresh.
- [ ] Snapshot models and restore code do not include agent status entries, agent PIDs, notifications, live process identity, transient loading state, or other ephemeral runtime state.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-o2m.1` — restore policy needs the full-session restore boundary and validation path.

## Notes for future agents

`WorkspaceManager.isRunningUnderAutomatedTests` already handles some test/env suppression. Expand this into an explicit launch restore policy that can be unit tested without depending on real `NSApplication` process state. The spec requires no schema migration: mismatched schema version discards the whole snapshot.

title: Phase 4: Capture and replay terminal scrollback safely
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-o2m`.
Reference user stories: 6, 7, 10, 11.

## What to build

Add terminal scrollback persistence for terminal panels. Capture up to 400,000 characters per terminal panel, truncate without replaying from the middle of an ANSI escape sequence, wrap truncated replay text in ANSI reset codes, and restore that scrollback into reopened terminal panels without corrupting terminal display state.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Terminal panel snapshots include scrollback text capped at 400,000 characters per panel.
- [ ] Truncation never starts replay inside a partial ANSI escape sequence.
- [ ] Truncated scrollback is wrapped with ANSI reset codes so colors/styles do not leak incorrectly into restored content.
- [ ] Restored terminal panels show the saved scrollback before or alongside the new shell in the intended Phase 4 behavior.
- [ ] Pure tests cover plain text, empty scrollback, exact-limit content, over-limit content, complete ANSI sequences, and partial ANSI sequences at the truncation boundary.
- [ ] Integration tests verify a workspace save/restore includes terminal scrollback for the right terminal panel.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-o2m.2` — terminal scrollback must attach to the restored terminal panel topology.

## Notes for future agents

Current terminal state is in `Argus/Ghostty/TerminalSurface.swift` and `Argus/Models/TerminalPanel.swift`; there is no scrollback capture API in the current wrapper. Prefer a deep, testable scrollback formatter/truncator module with a tiny Ghostty integration seam. Validate GhosttyKit capabilities before choosing how to capture/replay scrollback; keep failures safe and do not block quit indefinitely.

title: Phase 4: Persist typed browser panel state contract
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-o2m`.
Reference user stories: 8.

## What to build

Add the Phase 4 persistence contract for browser panels even if the full Browser Panels feature is implemented later. The session format should support typed browser panel snapshots with current URL, page zoom level, and developer tools visibility, and restoration should be routed through the same panel snapshot mechanism used by terminal panels.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Panel snapshots are typed and can round-trip terminal and browser panel payloads without ambiguity.
- [ ] Browser panel snapshots include URL, zoom level, and developer tools visibility.
- [ ] Unknown or unavailable browser panel restoration is safe and deterministic if browser UI support is not present yet.
- [ ] Tests cover Codable round trip for browser panel state and mixed terminal/browser panel ordering.
- [ ] The design does not require implementing the WKWebView browser UI as part of this slice.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-o2m.2` — typed panel snapshots should integrate with the restored panel ordering/focus model.

## Notes for future agents

Current `PanelType` already has `.browser`, but no `BrowserPanel` implementation exists in the file tree. This task is about the persistence contract and restoration extension point, not Phase 5 browser UI. Keep the schema durable and product-oriented: URL, zoom, developer tools visibility.

title: Phase 4: Autosave quiet sessions and save synchronously on quit
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-o2m`.
Reference user stories: 10, 11.

## What to build

Add a save policy that persists the full session automatically about every 8 seconds during typing-quiet periods and writes synchronously during application termination. The policy should avoid excessive writes during high-volume terminal activity while ensuring normal quit completes only after the session is durably written.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Mutations to workspace, panel, project, sidebar, and restorable panel state mark the session dirty.
- [ ] Autosave writes after an approximately 8-second quiet interval and coalesces rapid changes into a single save.
- [ ] Autosave does not block the main thread with file I/O during normal operation.
- [ ] `applicationWillTerminate` performs a synchronous save before termination continues.
- [ ] Autosave tests use a controllable scheduler/clock or equivalent seam rather than slow wall-clock sleeps.
- [ ] Save failures are reported/logged without crashing normal UI flows; quit-save behavior remains deterministic.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-o2m.1` — autosave needs the full-session save boundary.

## Notes for future agents

Current synchronous lifecycle hook is `AppDelegate.applicationWillTerminate`, which calls `WorkspaceManager.saveSession()`. The new policy should centralize save scheduling, likely outside `WorkspaceManager`, while keeping UI state mutations on the main thread and disk I/O off the main thread except for the required quit save.

title: Phase 4: Verify full-session behavior across save, quit, and relaunch
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-o2m`.
Reference user stories: 1 through 17.

## What to build

Add final Phase 4 verification coverage that exercises the complete persistence feature as a user would experience it: create or load a rich session, save it, simulate quit/relaunch, and verify the restored app state while confirming unsafe or ephemeral state is absent.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A rich session containing projects, catch-all workspace, multiple workspaces, multiple tabs, splits, sidebar state, selected workspace, selected panel, terminal directories, and scrollback restores correctly.
- [ ] Browser panel state snapshots are included in verification through the typed persistence contract or available browser implementation.
- [ ] Restore suppression cases still produce a fresh session in the end-to-end path.
- [ ] Ephemeral runtime state is not present after restore.
- [ ] The test suite documents Phase 4 prior art so future Phase 5/6 work can extend persistence without re-opening core session behavior.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 2

## Blocked by

- `argus-o2m.3` — final verification needs launch policy behavior.
- `argus-o2m.4` — final verification needs scrollback integration.
- `argus-o2m.5` — final verification needs typed browser panel snapshot support.
- `argus-o2m.6` — final verification needs save scheduling and quit-save behavior.

## Notes for future agents

Prefer deterministic unit/integration tests over brittle UI automation. Existing Swift tests are grouped by phase under `Tests/`; add Phase 4 tests near the persistence feature and reuse existing helpers/patterns from Phase 2 and Phase 3. Test behavior users care about, not incidental private representations.

title: PRD: Phase 2 project/worktree stabilization
description:
## Problem

Argus has partial Phase 2 project/worktree support, but important behavior is incomplete or incorrect. Projects are not durable across relaunch, restored workspace/project relationships are not reconciled, catch-all workspace creation is broken in the project sidebar, orphan adoption creates new worktrees instead of adopting existing ones, and repository/branch handling is not robust enough for daily use.

## Solution

Stabilize the Phase 2 project and git worktree workflow by adding minimal durable project/workspace persistence, restore reconciliation, correct catch-all UI behavior, true orphan adoption, canonical repository-root handling, robust duplicate detection, correct branch identity for main checkouts, and safer branch/worktree creation semantics.

## User Stories

1. As an Argus user, I want projects and workspaces to survive app relaunches, so that my workspace organization is not lost.
2. As an Argus user, I want restored project and workspace links to self-heal, so that sidebar hierarchy remains consistent after loading saved state.
3. As an Argus user, I want the catch-all project add button to create a standalone workspace, so that unassigned work remains easy to start.
4. As an Argus user, I want orphaned worktrees to be adopted in place, so that existing disk work is not duplicated or lost.
5. As an Argus user, I want branch-name collisions to be handled before worktree creation, so that creating a workspace does not fail late or damage expectations.
6. As an Argus user, I want project creation to normalize selected directories to the repository root, so that projects behave consistently even when I choose a subdirectory.
7. As an Argus user, I want duplicate-project detection to compare canonical repository roots, so that the same repository cannot be added twice through path aliases.
8. As an Argus user, I want main-checkout workspaces to show the branch currently checked out, so that sidebar/title context is accurate.
9. As an Argus user, I want existing local and remote branch checkout to create usable worktrees without detached or ambiguous state, so that workspaces start on the intended branch.

## Implementation Decisions

- Add minimal persistence for projects, workspaces, project membership, worktree path, branch name, workspace type, selected workspace, and sidebar expansion state before full Phase 4 scrollback/browser persistence.
- Add restore-time reconciliation that ensures exactly one catch-all project, removes stale references, attaches unassigned workspaces to catch-all, and preserves project workspace order where valid.
- Correct catch-all project UI actions to create standalone workspaces rather than project worktree workspaces.
- Adopt orphaned worktrees by creating workspace state pointing at the existing path rather than invoking worktree creation.
- Check branch existence and generate unique branch names where creating new project workspaces requires new branches.
- Resolve selected directories to canonical git repository roots before creating or comparing projects.
- Read the current checkout branch for main-checkout workspaces separately from the detected main branch.
- Handle existing remote branches by creating or tracking a suitable local branch before adding a worktree when needed.

## Testing Decisions

- Add isolated tests for repository-root normalization, duplicate detection, branch uniqueness, and worktree path adoption where feasible.
- Use temporary git repositories for integration-style `WorktreeService` tests when the local test environment supports git.
- Validate persistence through encode/decode or save/load tests that assert project/workspace cross-references after reconciliation.
- Manually smoke-test new project creation from a repo subdirectory, duplicate rejection through symlinks/path variants, orphan adoption, and existing remote branch worktree creation.

## Out of Scope

- Full Phase 4 session persistence including scrollback, browser state, autosave quiet-period behavior, and schema migration policy.
- Git status sidebar and file operations.
- CLI/socket project management commands.
- Multi-window support.

## Further Notes

The authoritative spec remains `.specs/argus-application.md`. Phase 2 fixes should preserve the required storage path shape `~/.argus/worktrees/<project-uuid>/<branch-slug>/` and keep project UUIDs as stable storage keys.

title: Phase 2: persist minimal project and workspace state
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-rgf`.
Reference user story 1.

## What to build

Add minimal durable persistence for Phase 2 project/workspace state so created projects, project workspace membership, workspace branch/worktree metadata, selected workspace, and project expansion state survive app relaunch.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Named projects persist and restore with UUID, repository path, display name, main branch, expansion state, color, catch-all flag, and workspace IDs.
- [ ] Workspaces persist and restore with project ID, worktree path, branch name, workspace type, custom title, current directory, and panel enough to reopen a terminal panel.
- [ ] Selected workspace is restored when valid, otherwise a valid workspace is selected.
- [ ] Incompatible or empty snapshots are discarded safely according to the spec direction.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 1

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately.

## Notes for future agents

This is not full Phase 4 scrollback/browser persistence. Keep it minimal but durable enough that Phase 2 orphan detection and projects work across relaunches.

title: Phase 2: reconcile restored project and workspace references
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-rgf`.
Reference user story 2.

## What to build

Add restore-time reconciliation for project-to-workspace and workspace-to-project cross-references so sidebar hierarchy remains valid after loading saved state.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Exactly one catch-all project exists after restore.
- [ ] Stale project workspace IDs that do not resolve to workspaces are removed.
- [ ] Workspaces with missing or invalid project IDs are attached to catch-all.
- [ ] Project workspace order is preserved for valid IDs.
- [ ] Reconciliation is safe to run multiple times.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 1

## Blocked by

- `argus-rgf.1` — reconciliation needs restored project/workspace snapshots to operate on.

## Notes for future agents

The spec explicitly requires bidirectional consistency after session restore. Keep this as a pure model/service operation where possible.

title: Phase 2: fix catch-all add workspace behavior
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-rgf`.
Reference user story 3.

## What to build

Fix catch-all project add actions so they create standalone workspaces instead of opening the project worktree workspace sheet or attempting project worktree creation.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Catch-all header plus button creates a standalone workspace immediately or opens an appropriate standalone workspace flow.
- [ ] Catch-all context menu add action creates a standalone workspace.
- [ ] No project worktree sheet is shown for catch-all.

title: Phase 2: adopt orphaned worktrees in place
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-rgf`.
Reference user story 4.

## What to build

Fix orphaned worktree adoption so Argus creates workspace state pointing at the existing orphaned worktree path instead of invoking new worktree creation.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Adopting an orphan creates a workspace whose working directory and worktree path are the orphan path.
- [ ] Adoption does not call `git worktree add` or create a duplicate worktree directory.
- [ ] Adopted workspace branch name comes from detected orphan metadata where available.
- [ ] Adopted workspace is added to the owning project, selected, and removed from the orphan dialog.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 1

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately.

## Notes for future agents

Current orphan sheet calls `addWorkspaceToProject`, which creates a new worktree. Add a dedicated adoption path in the workspace manager/service layer.

title: Phase 2: ensure new worktree branch names are unique
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-rgf`.
Reference user story 5.

## What to build

Generate or validate unique git branch names for new project workspaces before invoking `git worktree add -b`, not just unique filesystem slugs.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] New branch creation checks existing local and relevant remote branch names.
- [ ] Desired branch names that already exist receive a numeric suffix before worktree creation.
- [ ] Filesystem slug uniqueness remains intact and independent of branch-name uniqueness.
- [ ] User-facing errors distinguish branch collision from worktree creation failure when uniqueness cannot be resolved.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 1

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately.

## Notes for future agents

`WorktreeService.uniqueSlug` only avoids path collisions. The spec also requires unique branch names when the desired branch already exists.

title: PRD: Phase 1 foundation gap closure
description:
## Problem

Argus has a working terminal/workspace prototype, but the Phase 1 foundation is not yet compliant with the application spec. Core window, titlebar, sidebar, tab, workspace ordering, and target-structure behavior remains incomplete or fragile, which makes later project, git, persistence, browser, and agent features harder to build safely.

## Solution

Close the Phase 1 foundation gaps so Argus behaves as a single-window macOS terminal workspace manager with predictable titlebar/window state, reorderable tabs and workspaces, persisted sidebar layout preferences, correct sidebar width constraints, and a CLI target scaffold suitable for later socket-backed commands.

## User Stories

1. As an Argus user, I want the app to present exactly one main window, so that my terminal workspace is not duplicated or split across accidental windows.
2. As an Argus user, I want the custom titlebar to display the active workspace context, so that I can orient myself without inspecting the sidebar.
3. As an Argus user, I want Mission Control and the Dock to show the current workspace context in the window title, so that macOS window switching is meaningful.
4. As an Argus user, I want to reorder tabs within a workspace, so that terminal panels match my workflow.
5. As an Argus user, I want to reorder workspaces in the sidebar, so that Cmd+number selection and visual order match my priorities.
6. As an Argus user, I want sidebar visibility and widths to persist, so that the app restores my preferred layout.
7. As an Argus user, I want the left sidebar width constrained to 80 px through 33% of the window width, so that resizing remains usable at all window sizes.
8. As an Argus developer, I want the CLI target scaffold present, so that later socket commands can be implemented without reopening project structure decisions.

## Implementation Decisions

- Enforce single-window runtime despite SwiftUI scene defaults.
- Mount the custom titlebar in the main window chrome and derive displayed context from the selected workspace.
- Keep the underlying `NSWindow.title` synchronized with the same display context used by the titlebar.
- Implement tab drag-and-drop reordering within the selected workspace.
- Implement workspace drag-and-drop reordering in sidebar order, respecting the project/catch-all hierarchy where applicable.
- Persist sidebar visibility and width preferences using lightweight app preferences until full session persistence exists.
- Compute the left-sidebar maximum width from live window geometry rather than a hardcoded constant.
- Add a minimal `argus` CLI target/package scaffold without implementing the full socket protocol yet.

## Testing Decisions

- Prefer behavioral tests for pure state transitions where practical: workspace selection, tab order, workspace order, and persistence preference round trips.
- UI behavior should be validated by building the app and manually smoke-testing window count, titlebar content, drag/drop, and persisted layout.
- CLI scaffold validation should prove the target builds and can print help/version output without depending on an app socket.

## Out of Scope

- Git status sidebar behavior.
- Browser panels.
- Session snapshot/scrollback persistence.
- Socket server and full CLI command surface.
- Agent status and TTS.

## Further Notes

The authoritative spec remains `.specs/argus-application.md`. Keep the Phase 1 fixes simple and compatible with later Phase 2 project hierarchy and Phase 4 session persistence.

title: Phase 1: enforce single main window runtime
description:
## Source

Parent/source bead: `argus-t2d`.
Reference user story 1.

## What to build

Enforce the v1 invariant that Argus presents exactly one main window at runtime. Prevent or neutralize additional SwiftUI windows created by default scene behavior, while preserving normal launch, close, and reopen behavior for the single workspace window.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Argus cannot create multiple independent main workspace windows through standard macOS app/window actions.
- [ ] Closing the last window exits or restores according to the current app lifecycle policy without leaving duplicate hidden windows.
- [ ] The implementation is compatible with the existing `AppDelegate` window configuration path.
- [ ] Manual smoke test documents that relaunch/open/reopen produces one main window.

## Classification

- Slice type: AFK
- Beads type: task
- Priority: 1

## Blocked by

None — can start immediately.

## Notes for future agents

The spec requires a single main window in v1. Current app uses SwiftUI `WindowGroup`, which permits multiple windows by default. Keep the solution simple; do not introduce multi-window session machinery.

NEXT: done; manual smoke still requires launching app in a healthy Xcode/macOS environment. DECISIONS: replaced SwiftUI WindowGroup with unique Window("Argus", id: "main") to enforce one v1 main window while keeping AppDelegate configuration path.

title: Found while validation: bulk actions must handle capped hidden files
